exercises.title: Algorithm Evaluation Matrix
exercises.description: Create a comprehensive evaluation matrix comparing the NIST PQC finalists.
tasks: Create a matrix with ML-KEM, NTRU, SABER as columns
Include rows for: security proof quality, key sizes, ciphertext sizes, encaps speed, decaps speed, implementation complexity
Score each on a 1-5 scale with justification
Explain why ML-KEM achieved the highest overall score
Identify scenarios where NTRU might be preferred
exercises.expectedOutput: A scored comparison matrix with analysis and edge case recommendations
hints: SABER is very similar to Kyber

exercises.title: Security Proof Analysis
exercises.description: Research and explain the security proofs for ML-KEM.
tasks: Explain what the Module-LWE problem is
Describe how ML-KEM's security reduces to Module-LWE
Discuss what 'tightness' means for this reduction
Compare the security proof to that of NTRU
Explain why this matters for parameter selection
exercises.expectedOutput: A technical document explaining ML-KEM security proofs for advanced readers
hints: LWE involves distinguishing noisy linear equations from random
Module-LWE uses polynomial rings for efficiency

exercises.title: Side-Channel Vulnerability Assessment
exercises.description: Analyze the side-channel attack surface for each NIST PQC standard.
tasks: Identify the main side-channel risks for ML-KEM
Compare timing attack resistance across ML-DSA and SLH-DSA
Explain why FALCON is harder to implement securely
Propose mitigation strategies for each algorithm
Rank algorithms by ease of secure implementation
exercises.expectedOutput: A security assessment document with vulnerability analysis and mitigations
hints: Rejection sampling is a timing attack vector
Hash-based schemes have simpler side-channel profiles
Gaussian sampling in FALCON is particularly tricky

exercises.title: Future Algorithm Prediction
exercises.description: Based on NIST's selection criteria, predict which additional algorithms might be standardized.
tasks: Review the Round 4 candidates (BIKE, HQC, Classic McEliece)
Apply NIST's selection criteria to each
Predict which is most likely to be standardized next
Identify what would need to change for the others
Propose criteria for a hypothetical future PQC competition
exercises.expectedOutput: A prediction document with analysis based on established criteria
hints: Classic McEliece has huge keys but 45+ years of cryptanalysis
BIKE and HQC offer better performance than McEliece
